---
title: "Run and build the Study Knowledge Graph"
subtitle: "The R Shiny graph, from running it to building it yourself"
format:
  html:
    theme: darkly
    toc: true
    toc-location: left
    toc-title: On this page
    embed-resources: true
    highlight-style: github-dark
    code-copy: true
execute:
  eval: false
---

## Run it

```r
install.packages(c("shiny", "bslib", "visNetwork", "igraph"))   # once
shiny::runApp("knowledge_vault/shiny")
```

Filter by note type, click a note to read its detail and neighbours.

## The one principle: separate the logic from the UI

The graph logic lives in plain functions in `shiny/R/logic.R`. The app, the
[visual guide](kg-guide.html), the [interactive graph](kg-graph.html), and the unit
tests all call the same functions.

## Step 1 - the data is three CSVs

```
nodes.csv:  id, title, type, status, owner, cluster, summary
edges.csv:  from, to
types.csv:  type, label, color
```

The three CSVs *are* the vault. Add a note as a row, wire it up with edges, and it
appears in the graph. Nothing about the study is hard-coded.

## Step 2 - the logic turns CSVs into a graph

`igraph` gives you the metrics for free - degree (how connected), betweenness (which
note brokers between clusters):

```r
kg_igraph <- function(kg)
  igraph::simplify(igraph::graph_from_data_frame(
    kg$edges[, c("from","to")], directed = FALSE, vertices = kg$nodes["id"]))

kg_metrics <- function(kg) {
  g <- kg_igraph(kg); n <- kg$nodes
  n$degree      <- as.integer(igraph::degree(g)[n$id])
  n$betweenness <- as.integer(round(igraph::betweenness(g, normalized = TRUE)[n$id] * 100))
  n
}
```

`kg_vis()` then shapes the nodes/edges for `visNetwork` (colour by type, size by degree,
an HTML tooltip per note).

## Step 3 - the app draws it with visNetwork

```r
output$net <- renderVisNetwork({
  visNetwork(sub()$nodes, sub()$edges, background = "#141925") |>
    visOptions(highlightNearest = list(enabled = TRUE, degree = 1, hover = TRUE)) |>
    visPhysics(solver = "forceAtlas2Based") |>
    visLegend(useGroups = FALSE, addNodes = legend) |>
    visEvents(selectNode =
      "function(n){ Shiny.setInputValue('kg_sel', n.nodes[0], {priority:'event'}); }")
})
```

The type checkboxes drive `sub()` (a filtered nodes/edges list), so re-checking a type
re-draws the subgraph. Clicking a node fires `visEvents` -> `input$kg_sel`, which the
detail panel reads.

## Step 4 - the detail panel and metrics

`kg_neighbors(kg, id)` lists the notes a selected note links to; `kg_stats(kg)` gives
the value-box numbers (notes, links, clusters, the hub). All from `igraph`.

## Verify it

```r
Rscript knowledge_vault/tests/test-logic.R     # 28 notes, 59 links, hub is most-connected
```

## Make it yours

- **Add a note:** a row in `nodes.csv` + its links in `edges.csv`.
- **Add a category:** a row in `types.csv` (type, label, colour).
- **Change what "important" means:** size nodes by `betweenness` instead of `degree` in `kg_vis()`.

Same principle as the other consoles (logic core, data as CSV, one render path):
the [lifecycle monitor](../../lifecycle_dashboard/quarto/lifecycle-build.html) and the
[risk console](../../risk_monitor/quarto/risk-build.html).
